text: 10 Talks Are Recorded Live at the Ted Conference.

text: This episode features cognitive scientist Deb Roy.

text: This talk contains powerful visuals.
label: Positive

text: Download the video at Ted.com.

text: Here's Deb Roy.
label: Neutral

text: Imagine if you could record your life.
label: Neutral

text: Everything you said, everything you did.
label: Neutral

text: Available in a perfect memory store at your fingertips.
label: Positive

text: So you could go back and find memorable moments and relive them.
label: Neutral

text: Or sift through traces of time and discover patterns
label: Neutral

text: in your own life that previously had gone undiscovered.
label: Neutral

text: Well, that's exactly the journey that my family began
label: Positive

text: five and a half years ago.
label: Neutral

text: This is my wife and collaborator, Rupal.
label: Neutral

text: And on this day, at this moment, we
label: Neutral

text: walked into the house with our first child, our beautiful baby boy.
label: Positive

text: And we walked into a house with a very special home video recording system.
label: Positive

text: Thank you so much.
label: Positive

text: Hey, baby.
label: Neutral

text: OK.
label: Positive

text: This moment and thousands of other moments special for us
label: Positive

text: were captured in our home because in every room in the house,
label: Neutral

text: if you looked up, you'd see a camera and a microphone.
label: Neutral

text: And if you look down, you get this bird's eye view of the room.
label: Neutral

text: Here's our living room, the baby bedroom,
label: Neutral

text: kitchen, dining room, and the rest of the house.
label: Neutral

text: And all of these fed into a disc array that
label: Neutral

text: was designed for a continuous capture.
label: Neutral

text: So here we are flying through a day in our home
label: Neutral

text: as we move from sunlight morning through incandescent evening
label: Neutral

text: and finally lights out for the day.
label: Neutral

text: Over the course of three years, we recorded eight to 10 hours a day,
label: Neutral

text: amassing roughly a quarter million hours of multi-track audio and video.
label: Neutral

text: So you're looking at a piece of what
label: Neutral

text: is, by far, the largest home video collection ever made.
label: Neutral

text: I think that's clear.
label: Positive

text: And what this data represents for our family at a personal level,
label: Neutral

text: the impact has already been immense and we're still learning its value.
label: Positive

text: Countless moments of unsolicited natural moments, not posed moments,
label: Positive

text: are captured there.
label: Neutral

text: And we're starting to learn how to discover them and find them.
label: Neutral

text: But there's also a scientific reason that drove this project, which
label: Neutral

text: was to use this kind of natural longitudinal data
label: Positive

text: to understand the process of how a child learns language, that child being my son.
label: Neutral

text: And so with many privacy provisions put in place to protect everyone
label: Positive

text: who's recorded in the data, we made elements of the data available
label: Neutral

text: to my trusted research team at MIT so we could start teasing
label: Positive

text: apart patterns in this massive data set, trying to understand
label: Neutral

text: the influence of social environments on language acquisition.
label: Neutral

text: So we're looking here at one of the first things we started to do.
label: Neutral

text: This is my wife and I cooking breakfast in the kitchen.
label: Neutral

text: And as we move through space and through time, a very everyday pattern of life
label: Neutral

text: in the kitchen, in order to convert this opaque 90,000 hours of video into something
label: Neutral

text: we can start to see, we use motion analysis to pull out as we move through space
label: Neutral

text: and through time, what we call space-time worms.
label: Neutral

text: And this has become a part of our toolkit for being able to look and see
label: Neutral

text: where the activities are in the data.
label: Neutral

text: And with it, trace the patterns of in particular where my son moved throughout the home.
label: Neutral

text: So we could focus our transcription efforts, all the speech environment around my son,
label: Neutral

text: all the words that he heard from myself, my wife, our nanny,
label: Neutral

text: and over time the words he began to produce.
label: Neutral

text: So with that technology and that data and the ability to, with machine assistance,
label: Positive

text: transcribe speech, we've now transcribed well over 7 million words of our home transcripts.
label: Positive

text: And with that, let me take you now for a first tour into the data.
label: Neutral

text: So you've all, I'm sure, seen time lapse videos where a flower will blossom as you accelerate time.
label: Positive

text: I'd like you to now experience the blossoming of a speech form.
label: Positive

text: My son, soon after his first birthday, would say Gaga to mean water.
label: Neutral

text: And over the course of the next half year, he slowly learns to approximate the proper adult form water.
label: Neutral

text: So we're going to cruise through half a year in about 40 seconds.
label: Neutral

text: Look, kid.

text: Blow her.

text: Are you sure nailed it, didn't they?

text: Applause.

text: So he didn't just learn water over the course of the 24 months,
label: Neutral

text: the first two years that we really focused on, this is a map of every word he learned in chronological order.
label: Positive

text: And because we have full transcripts, we've identified each of the 503 words that he learned to produce by his second birthday.
label: Neutral

text: He was an early talker.
label: Neutral

text: And so we started to analyze why, why were certain words born before others?
label: Positive

text: This is one of the first results that came out of our study a little over a year ago that really surprised us.
label: Positive

text: The way to interpret this apparently simple graph is on the vertical is the indication of how complex caregiver utterances are based on the length of utterances.
label: Neutral

text: And the vertical axis is time.
label: Neutral

text: And all of the data we aligned based on the following idea.
label: Neutral

text: Every time my son would learn a word, we would trace back and look at all of the language he heard that contained that word.
label: Neutral

text: And we would plot the relative length of the utterances.
label: Neutral

text: And what we found was this curious phenomena that caregiver speech would systematically dip to a minimum, making language as simple as possible, and then slowly a sand backup in complexity.
label: Positive

text: And the amazing thing was that that bounce, that dip, lined up almost precisely with when each word was born.
label: Positive

text: Word after word systematically.
label: Neutral

text: So it appears that all three primary caregivers, myself, my wife and our nanny, were systematically and I would think subconsciously restructuring our language to meet him at the moment of the birth of a word and bring him gently into more complex language.
label: Positive

text: And the implications of this, there are many, but one I just want to point out is that there must be amazing feedback loops.
label: Positive

text: It's not, of course, my son is learning from his linguistic environment, but the environment is learning from him.
label: Neutral

text: That environment, people are in these tight feedback loops and creating a kind of scaffolding that has not been noticed until now.
label: Positive

text: But that's looking at the speech context, what about the visual context?
label: Neutral

text: We're now looking at, think of this as a dollhouse cut away of our house.
label: Negative

text: We've taken those circular fish islands cameras and we've done some optical correction and then we can bring it into three dimensional life.
label: Neutral

text: So welcome to my home.
label: Positive

text: This is a moment, one moment captured across multiple cameras.
label: Neutral

text: The reason we did this is to create the ultimate memory machine where you can go back and interactively fly around and then breathe video life into this system.
label: Positive

text: What I'm going to do is give you an accelerated view of 30 minutes, again, of just life in the living room.
label: Neutral

text: That's me and my son on the floor.
label: Neutral

text: And there's video analytics that are tracking our movements.
label: Neutral

text: My son is leaving red ink.
label: Neutral

text: I'm leaving green ink.
label: Neutral

text: We're now in the couch looking out through the window at cars passing by and finally my son playing in a walking toy by himself.
label: Positive

text: Now we freeze the action 30 minutes.
label: Positive

text: We turn time into the vertical axis and we open up for a view of these interaction traces we've just left behind.
label: Neutral

text: And we see these amazing structures.
label: Positive

text: These little knots of two colors of thread we call social hotspots.
label: Neutral

text: The spiral thread we call a solo hotspot.
label: Neutral

text: And we think that these affect the way language is learned.
label: Neutral

text: What we'd like to do is start understanding the interaction between these patterns and the language that my son is exposed to.
label: Positive

text: To see if we can predict how the structure of when words are heard affects when they're learned.
label: Neutral

text: So in other words, the relationship between words and what they're about in the world.
label: Neutral

text: So here's how we're approaching this.
label: Neutral

text: In this video, again, my son is being traced out.
label: Neutral

text: He's leaving red ink behind and there's our nanny by the door.
label: Neutral

text: Do you want water?
label: Positive

text: No.
label: Negative

text: All right.
label: Neutral

text: No.
label: Negative

text: She offers water.
label: Neutral

text: And off go the two worms over to the kitchen to get water.
label: Neutral

text: And what we've done is use the word water to tag that moment, that bit of activity.
label: Neutral

text: And now we take the power of data and take every time my son ever heard the word water and the context he saw it in.
label: Neutral

text: And we use it to penetrate through the video and find every activity trace that co-occurred with an instance of water.
label: Neutral

text: And what this data leaves in its weight is a landscape.
label: Neutral

text: We call these wordscapes.
label: Neutral

text: This is the word scape for the word water.
label: Neutral

text: And you can see most of the action is in the kitchen.
label: Neutral

text: That's where those big peaks are over to the left.
label: Neutral

text: And just for contrast, we can do this with any word.
label: Neutral

text: We can take the word buy as in goodbye.
label: Neutral

text: And we're now zoomed in over the entrance to the house.
label: Neutral

text: And we look and we find as you expect a contrast in the landscape where the word buy
label: Neutral

text: occurs much more in a structured way.
label: Neutral

text: So we're using these structures to start predicting the order of language acquisition.
label: Neutral

text: And that's sort of ongoing work now.
label: Neutral

text: In my lab, which we're peering into now at MIT.
label: Neutral

text: This is at the media lab.
label: Neutral

text: This has become my favorite way of videographing just about any space.
label: Positive

text: Three of the key people in this project, Philip DeCamp, Ronnie Kubat, and Brandon Royer, are pictured here.
label: Neutral

text: Philip has been a close collaborator on all the visualizations you're seeing.
label: Neutral

text: And Michael Fleischman was another PhD student in my lab who worked with me on this home video analysis.
label: Neutral

text: And he made the following observation that just the way that we're analyzing how language connects
label: Neutral

text: to events which provide common ground for language, that same idea.
label: Neutral

text: We can take out of your home, Deb, and we can apply it to the world of public media.
label: Neutral

text: And so our effort took on an expected turn.
label: Neutral

text: Think of mass media as providing common ground.
label: Neutral

text: And you have the recipe for taking this idea to a whole new place.
label: Neutral

text: We've started analyzing television content using the same principles, analyzing event structure of a TV signal,
label: Neutral

text: episodes of shows, commercials, all of the components that make up the event structure.
label: Neutral

text: And we're now with satellite dishes pulling in and analyzing a good part of all the TV being watched in the United States.
label: Positive

text: And you don't have to now go and instrument living rooms with microphones to get people's conversations.
label: Neutral

text: You just tune in to publicly available social media feeds.
label: Neutral

text: So we're pulling in about three billion comments a month.
label: Neutral

text: And then the magic happens.
label: Neutral

text: You have the event structure, the common ground that the words are about coming out of the television feeds.
label: Neutral

text: You've got the conversations that are about that, those topics.
label: Neutral

text: And through semantic analysis, and this is actually real data you're looking at,
label: Neutral

text: from our data, our processing, each yellow line is showing a link being made between a comment in the wild
label: Neutral

text: and a piece of event structure coming out of the television signal.
label: Neutral

text: And the same idea now can be built up.
label: Neutral

text: And we get this wordscape, except now words are not assembled in my living room.
label: Neutral

text: Instead the context, the common ground, the activities are the content on television that's driving the conversations.
label: Neutral

text: And so what we're seeing here, these skyscrapers now, are commentary that are linked to content on television.
label: Neutral

text: Same concept, but looking at communication dynamics in a different, very different sphere.
label: Positive

text: So fundamentally, rather than, for example, measuring content based on how many people are watching,
label: Neutral

text: this gives us the basic data for looking at engagement properties of content.
label: Positive

text: And just like we can look at feedback cycles and dynamics in a family,
label: Positive

text: we can now open up the same concepts and look at much larger groups of people.
label: Neutral

text: This is a subset of data from our database, just 50,000 out of several million,
label: Neutral

text: and the social graph that connects them through publicly available sources.
label: Neutral

text: And if you put them on one plane, a second plane is where the content lives.
label: Neutral

text: So we have the programs and the sporting events and the commercials and all of the link structures

text: that tie them together make a content graph.
label: Neutral

text: And then the important third dimension, each of the links that you're seeing rendered here
label: Positive

text: is an actual connection made between something someone said and a piece of content.
label: Neutral

text: And there are again, now tens of millions of these links that give us the connective tissue of social graphs
label: Neutral

text: and how they relate to content.
label: Neutral

text: And we can now start to probe the structure in interesting ways.
label: Positive

text: So if we, for example, trace the path of one piece of content that drives someone to comment on it.

label: Neutral

text: Very interesting structure becomes visible.
label: Positive

text: We call this a co-viewing clique, a virtual living room, if you will.
label: Neutral

text: And there are fascinating dynamics that play.
label: Positive

text: It's not one way, a piece of content and a vent causes someone to talk.
label: Neutral

text: They talk to other people that drives tune in behavior back into mass media.
label: Neutral

text: And you have these cycles that drive the overall behavior.

label: Neutral

text: we can x-ray and get a real time pulse of a nation, real time sense of the social reactions

text: So to summarize, the idea is this,
label: Neutral

text: as our world becomes increasingly instrumented,
label: Neutral

text: and we have the capabilities to collect and connect the dots
label: Neutral

text: between what people are saying and the context they're saying in.
label: Neutral

text: What's emerging is an ability to see new social structures and dynamics
label: Positive

text: that have previously not been seen.
label: Neutral

text: It's like building a microscope or a telescope and revealing new structures

text: about our own behavior around communication.
label: Neutral

text: And I think the implications here are profound,
label: Neutral

text: whether it's for science, for commerce, for government,
label: Neutral
